item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I should probably be crankier about the Foundation making a leap of logic ("red traffic light = faustian papist"), but I feel like it actually works alright in shorter articles like this. I also appreciate that the Foundation is doing an honest to God experiment to test whether this is indeed connected.

If there's one complaint to be had, I didn't really get why the second circle was specifically mentioned, as Hell's horny jail is, iirc, thematically connected with dissolute behavior and violent but temporary passions, not the sort of thing you'd associate with "i will kill myself and sell my soul to a/the devil to see papal domionion stretch from horizon to horizon."

Overall, though, as good as I remember. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it is owned by Walter Kulash as indicated here. Thus, the image has been removed.

The second image has been deemed to be user-generated, and thus is sourced as follows:

Source: SCP Foundation Wiki
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: unnamed-2.jpg
Author: Kate McTiriss
Release year: 2014

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted purely for the Swaziland/Vatican war and attempted peace treaty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute. I like it. And the connection does not feel forced to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every article that sparcles debate in community is noteworthy. That being said, I don't like this one. Connection between a traffic light and a pact is rather obvious to me, yet I feel that the article is lacking cohesion.

Now, maybe I'm missing something here, but why would a good deacon sell his soul to the devil for something like that? I don't think Vatican actually took active part in any wars since, well, medieval I guess? Of course that could be a metaphor for catholicism falling before other religions, but I don't see this happening any time soon. This gesture feels like a last resort, not something to be done just because. Then again, maybe deacon was simply nuts. Which does not make for a very interesting story.

Next one. Deacon asks Asmodeus to take his soul to the "Second Circle", presumably that of Hell, with Asmodeus being the King of Nine Hells and all that. Why second though? It is a circle usually associated with lust and Asmodeus himself is a demon of lust, but how does lust tie in here? It feels like there should be an interesting backstory there, but there isn't one and there are no hints at it.

I also don't get why this thing has to be at that exact place. Obviously it doesn't need power or anything else to operate. And it's not like deacon commited suicide on this crossroad. As I understand, author was writing this after a thing from real life, and this shaped the article to some degree, resulting in the fact that Foundation can't secure that traffic light. The reality-bending effect feels forced, just a weak explanation why we couldn't take this thing away and lock it up.

Addendum is somewhat amusing but I personally would prefer if it documented an already conducted experiment, maybe even with result being redacted or referencing another document. Articles "in progress" just don't sit right with me because these plans don't add anything informative and scp articles are supposed to be short informative summaries, right?
Also, I don't like reality measuring and don't see anything bad with one or two expungements, because at least they leave something to your imagination.

Overall this feels like a mish-mash of different stories and ideas loosedly tied together. I think this article could become much better if you added more backstory to it, something that would connect all the dots.
As for now, it's -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The world is burning. RUN.

item.title: how i imagine it
item.content:encoded:
" Oh lord, give us an eternal flame, which shall be a beacon that Catholicism can never be destroyed."
Dies, and his ghost looks on the world.
"…A stoplight? That's our beacon? Are you f…"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like those above me, I seriously hate the idea of measuring reality. It suggests that the Foundation has a firm, absolute understanding of our universe, and aren't sharing that ridiculously useful information with the world. We're talking about scientists, not a conspiracy theorist's version of the U.S. government.
A way to fix this would be to explain what exactly happens, like fluctuations in space-time, or mass memetic mutations, or a change in the laws of physics within the area.

Other than that, though, a very well done SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs to attribute some anomalous property to -2 for it to be classified as a part of the SCP, and some observable connection to -1 for it to be in the same article. It then needs to end right after the excerpt from the journal, because the addendum describing future testing does nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has no reliance on other entries in the DNM series. I understand your perspective, but a significant majority of voters understand the connection and chose to upvote the article. I don't intend to expand it at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get the SCP even after several rereads. Well, I understand what is going on, but it really does feel like two articles merged together with little rhyme or reason. I don't understand the dixieland nor the Humes backstory (the only one I know is Desmond Hume and Hume the philosopher) and such have to downvote because of needed reliance on other established articles.
Also reality levels? Really?

I mean, even a crosslink would work better than grasping at straws.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Inetersting read, but I have a minor Hebrew nitpick about the picture - the kamatz (the T-like marking) should be under the daled (ד), not under the alef (א) in the second and third letters from the left in the word אשמדאי, otherwise it is pronounced "ash-med-aai" instead of the correct "ash-me-dai".

Apart from that, love the proposed experiment. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the one hand, knowing it turned green again 14 years ago, I'm almost left too curious about the ramifications to upvote. On the other hand, I have too much of a weakness for Weird Florida Things not to upvote it, so I'm a bit torn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. I really like the infernal red light, but the test sounds silly. If the test war is a sham the result would be non conclusive. Moreover, a devil with that kind of twisted sense of humor & logic could interpret ''war'' and ''victory'' in just about any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's what turns this article from just a decent one into a great one.

I agree with Trinitite. The test is odd at first but clever upon consideration, giving insight into how the Foundation might try to understand and manipulate an anomalous object and its properties.

That somebody at the Foundation managed to find the journal and connect it to the stoplight at all is interesting, but the test itself (as a result of the journal) is really what makes the article for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the connection between the notebook and the stop light was fairly obvious on the first read. The entire Dixieland Nightmare Magic series is fantastic, please keep writing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, using Humes instead of expungement is just trading one bad idea for another, honestly. But you're right that it has no bearing on the article overall, and that's why the inclusion didn't keep me from an upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I didn't get it.
Then I looked at the scrawled symbol in the guy's notebook and was like, "That kinda resembles a stop light," and then it clicked and I upvoted with the force of a supernova.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a tenuous link. Aside from Hume use, the most common complaint of this SCP is some form of "why are two separate articles merged like this?"

If the link was stated a bit more clearer, it wouldn't be an issue: simply adding something along the lines of "based on the information in the Deacan's note" in the addendum should handle it. It concretely links the stoplight to the test, but doesn't really give away what's going on. At the very least, it will make the reader scrutinize the note more carefully for its meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't actually know it was a common funny picture! I'm from the city that stoplight was in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah….I don't like humes.. the idea that reality can be "measured" is just a ludicrous notion to me. That and it feels like a massive non sequitur between a dude killing himself and a magic traffic light unless you reread the damn thing like 8 times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first thought in my head the moment I saw the article was "if this is using a common internet funny picture, and has a +45, it must be incredibly good." (I'd seen that picture quite a number of times before.)

And after reading it, that definitely seems to be the case. A reason has been added for its existence through some backstory - and its impossibility of usual containment and status as a local tourist attraction gives it plausible reason to be a commonly-found picture.

That aside, I am able to understand what's going on in the article, and it has cohesiveness; everything ties together well. If you're planning to eventually do the test, I'm looking forward to the results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, this use is fine. I mean, I'd be absolutely happy for it if not for the dreadful guides - there's at least one other place mystery units of faith measurement (the famed centiakivas) were used way, way before someone tried to explain them, and that was fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're still better than expungement, but they still feel too much like foundation midichlorians for me to like. The rest of the article was good, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great comment. "We can't do [thing that would be an obvious act of simple containment] because DATA EXPUNGED" is becoming a bit of an easy way out. I'm glad to see you did something a bit different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it's not. It's what turns this article from just a decent one into a great one.

The Deacon asked for an eternal, symbolic flame. The demon gave him a traffic light that only shows red. The implication is that the demon might not be particularly good at its job, or maybe has a sense of humour about how it honours its obligations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is truly excellent. The idea of a sign of the Vatican's infallibility in the form of a red traffic light is just perfect, and the idea for a test is very nice. Perfectly executed.

My feeling is if they try it something terrible might happen to the King, but then again a demon who creates an eternal flame as a traffic light is probably rather low-rent, so…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The simple fact behind my decision to use Humes here is that 2172's reality destabilization is wholly irrelevant to the object's interest. It's a good reason for the Foundation to keep it hanging instead of putting it in a boring old box, and that's all it needs to do.

[EDIT: A bit more on what I mean by this: Humes are a fundamental part of my writing toolkit in this continuity because to otherwise describe what happens when this object is taken out of an area would take an expungement (ugly, cliché) or a hundred or so words on what exactly happens, which would distract from the focus of the piece.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. The Hume thing really sucks out the fun of knowing just how much reality is being warped. Yes, the Foundation loves measurements and objectivity, but I couldn't care less as a reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I officially hate the "we can measure levels of reality in an area" concept, but between the first image, the fate of a man who should have known better, and one of the most bizarre ideas for a test I have ever seen, I can't not upvote this.

item.title: Re: confusion
item.content:encoded:
Downvote removed after explanation and a re-read.

No upvote, though, because the link is still tenuous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn it. Now all I can imagine is anomalous boners. This is your fault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Humes are a tremendous tool for quantifying "weird shit happens around X." The Foundation loves nothing more than objectivity (or the appearance thereof), so being able to put a number to unreality was a breakthrough that probably gave every Regional Director at least a half-chub.

item.title: Re: confusion
item.content:encoded:
"So a guy killed himself, requesting a 'light that will never be extinguished' from a demon. On the same day, a red light that never goes out appeared in the same part of the world."

"Yeah."

"I just can't see the connection."

item.title: Re: confusion
item.content:encoded:
I think it is because King Asmodei is trapped within the stoplight.

E: Got it wrong, it is actually the flame the deacon requested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why do you do this, using Humes in a way that still makes me upvote because they don't horribly trivialize anything.

Seriously though, I love this - especially the poor Deacon whose wish might have been interpreted literally, and since Vatican doesn't really go into wars these days…

item.title: Re: confusion
item.content:encoded:
Agreed.

It's like you were working on the stoplight one… and then forgot about it to do something with the Vatican instead. And forgot to delete the first half.

item.title: confusion
item.content:encoded: I am not seeing the connection between the stoplight and the journalpact/vatican. Downvoted because it confused me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right. I redacted it to future-proof the article a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm feeling currently neutral on this. The concept is good, most of the execution is excellent, but I feel there's something missing from the description.

And as a slight pet peeve, I think directly referencing the sitting pope and king of Swaziland is a bit inconvenient for a non-imminent date (which it could be, granted), because either of them could die beforehand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Golden, as usual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't wonder where we're going
Or remember where we've been
We've got to keep this traffic
Flowing and accept a little spin
So this long line of cars
will never have an end
- "Long Line of Cars" by Cake

A new Dixieland Nightmare Magic skip based on a peculiar local artifact from near my hometown.
